title: Re-webbing and re-foaming a mid-century armchair seat
subtitle: Replace the webbing base and seat foam on a platform-seat armchair with a solid timber or ply seat rail frame
excerpt: How to re-web and re-foam the seat of a mid-century modern armchair with a platform seat. Covers stripping the old webbing and foam, tensioning new jute webbing across the rails, cutting new foam to size, and wrapping in upholstery wadding before re-covering.
sourceNotes: Upholstery: A Beginner's Guide, Alex Law (2010). City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery. UK traditional upholstery trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A seat construction in which the fabric top cover of the seat cushion area is stitched to a cloth base panel that sits on the webbing, forming a flat platform. The seat cushion is loose (a separate boxed cushion that sits on the platform. The platform hides the webbing and foam from view when the cushion is removed. Common on mid-century modern armchairs and sofas; the platform cover must be removed first to access the webbing beneath.

content.text: Re-webbing and re-foaming a mid-century armchair with a

restores a sunken armchair seat to its original shape. Strip out the old webbing, fit new jute webbing across the frame, cut new foam to fit, wrap it in upholstery wadding, and re-cover the platform panel.
Strip the old platform and webbing

Remove the platform cover panel by pulling out the staples or tacks around the perimeter with the upholstery staple remover. Remove the foam if it is simply sitting on the webbing. Strip all the old webbing using the staple remover) work systematically from one side to the other. The seat frame rails should be clean, solid timber with no cracks or loose joints before re-webbing.

Cut jute webbing strips to the seat depth plus 50 mm. Fold the leading end back 25 mm to double the thickness. Staple the folded end to the front rail with six staples in two rows of three. Use the webbing stretcher to tension the strip (the stretched length should be 10 to 15 per cent shorter than the unstretched strip. Staple the rear end, trim to 25 mm, fold back and staple again. Weave the cross strips through the front-to-back runs. Aim for strips at 50 to 60 mm centres across the full seat.

Cut the new foam to match the inner seat dimensions) exactly to the rail faces, not the outer rail edges. Use a 40 or 45 kg density seat foam, 50 to 75 mm thick. Cut with an electric carving knife or a long-bladed bread knife for a clean edge. Apply adhesive to the top face of the webbing and the foam base; press the foam down onto the webbing. No staples through the foam: it tears at the staple holes.
Wrap in upholstery wadding and re-fit the platform

Cut a piece of upholstery wadding to wrap over the top and tuck down each side of the foam. Pull snug and staple the tuck-down edges to the rail underside. The upholstery wadding softens the foam top edge and rounds the corners of the seat profile. Re-fit the platform cover panel over the upholstery wadding-wrapped foam, pulling the fabric drum-tight on all four sides and stapling to the seat rails at 50 mm intervals. Start at the centre front and back before working the sides.
